name: Kona Coast

name: Channel Islands

name: Jardines de la Reina
description: Main liveaboard window outside the peak storm season.

name: Silver Bank
description: Peak humpback interaction season in the North Atlantic.

name: Anse Chastanet
description: Drier Caribbean season generally offers the easiest boat and shore conditions.

name: Speyside
description: Favored season for Tobago drift diving before wetter summer conditions.

name: Larnaca
description: Warm Mediterranean wreck season with calmest seas and best visibility from late spring through autumn.

name: Salt Island
description: Dry season Caribbean window for the BVI's protected wreck and reef diving outside the late summer hurricane months.

name: Great Barrier Reef
description: The world's largest coral reef system, stretching over 2,300km off Queensland. Back-to-back mass bleaching events in 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, and 2024 have transformed large sections. Diving here documents what survives and where recovery is taking hold.

name: Malmok
description: Calm leeward bay off northwest Aruba sheltered from the trades, home to the WWII Antilla wreck and several smaller artificial reefs accessible by boat.

name: Scapa Flow
description: A wide, sheltered anchorage in Orkney, Scotland, holding the scuttled remains of the German High Seas Fleet from 1919 — the world's largest collection of divable WWI warships.

name: Tela
description: Coral cover above 60 percent when the Caribbean average has fallen below 20. Sea urchin populations here survived a 1983 pathogen that wiped out 95 percent of them everywhere else in the region. Scientists from the University of Miami, Operation Wallacea, and the Coral Reef Alliance are actively studying why — and early DNA work in 2024 found an unusually heat tolerant symbiotic algae inside Tela's elkhorn corals that is extremely rare elsewhere. Researchers are now crossbreeding Tela's elkhorn corals with Florida's nearly extinct populations to carry that resilience to reefs far beyond Honduras.

name: Wakatobi
description: A UNESCO Biosphere Reserve at the heart of the Coral Triangle, Wakatobi is one of Indonesia's most intact reef systems. Operation Wallacea has run continuous scientific monitoring here since 1998 and documented steady coral recovery following a 2010 bleaching event. Hard coral cover held at nearly 30 percent park-wide in 2019 surveys while destructive fishing declined. In 2024 when 84 percent of the world's reefs experienced bleaching-level heat stress, Wakatobi showed no significant bleaching — an anomaly researchers attribute to locally evolved heat tolerance in the coral colonies.

name: Sogod Bay
description: Sogod Bay hosts the second largest whale shark aggregation in Asia, with LAMAVE researchers having identified more than 350 individual sharks over a decade of monitoring. The outer reefs around Panaon Island reached 60 to 70 percent live coral cover in 2020 Oceana surveys — roughly three times the Philippine national average. Panaon Island was formally designated a Protected Seascape under national law in August 2025. Few dive operators work here and crowds are minimal even during peak whale shark season from November through April.

name: Derawan Archipelago
description: Derawan is one of the only places on Earth where reef manta rays are resident year-round. The Marine Megafauna Foundation has identified 155 individual mantas here — and 24 percent of the females have been observed pregnant, the only site in East Borneo where this is documented. With 500 plus coral species and 800 plus reef fish species the archipelago sits in the highest-diversity marine zone on the planet. The 285,549 hectare Berau MPA was declared in 2016 and active enforcement patrols with radar monitoring began in 2023.

name: Dahab
description: Dahab sits on the Sinai Peninsula where desert cliffs meet the electric blue Red Sea, and the diving here is legendary! The Blue Hole draws freedivers and scuba divers from every corner of the world, a cathedral sinkhole that plunges beyond 100 m alongside a coral wall of extraordinary beauty. The Bells and The Canyon offer adrenaline drift descents and sculpted swim-throughs that will stay with you long after you surface. Water temperatures are comfortable year round, and the town itself is one of the most relaxed and welcoming dive bases anywhere.

name: Sharm el Sheikh
description: Sharm el Sheikh anchors the southern tip of the Sinai Peninsula and has earned its place as one of the world's great dive destinations. Ras Mohammed National Park protects some of the most spectacular coral architecture in the Red Sea, and the WWII wreck of the SS Thistlegorm is widely regarded as the finest dive wreck on the planet. Expect schooling barracuda, resident sharks, and coral walls draped in soft corals of every color imaginable. The infrastructure here is world class, making it an ideal hub for liveaboard departures into the wider Red Sea.

name: Hurghada
description: Hurghada stretches along the Egyptian Red Sea coast and serves as the gateway to a remarkable collection of offshore reefs and historic wrecks. Giftun Island offers shallow coral gardens teeming with reef fish, while the notorious Abu Nuhas reef has claimed 4 ships whose rusting hulls now bristle with marine life. The reef at Shaab Abu Ramada is so rich with color and fish density that locals call it the Aquarium. Whether you are a first-time diver or an experienced wreck hunter, Hurghada delivers something extraordinary.

name: Pemba Island
description: An almost entirely undived island north of Zanzibar, ringed by walls that drop to beyond sport diving limits with zero crowds. Pemba sits in the Zanzibar Archipelago yet receives a fraction of its famous neighbour's dive traffic, which means healthy hard coral gardens, dense schools of fish, and encounters with large pelagics that feel genuinely wild. The steep walls are draped in gorgonians and black coral, and the currents that sweep the channels bring in barracuda, eagle rays, and occasional hammerhead sharks. This is frontier diving at its finest!

name: Puerto Galera
description: An easy day trip from Manila that punches above its weight with wrecks, walls, and world class macro in one sheltered bay on Mindoro's north coast.

name: Anilao
description: Batangas province's answer to Lembeh and considered the cradle of Philippine diving, Anilao's rocky reefs hold extraordinary nudibranch and macro density just 3 hours south of Manila.

name: El Nido, Palawan
description: Hidden inside Palawan's limestone karst wilderness, El Nido rewards explorers with pristine reefs, sea turtles, and manta encounters in the heart of the Coral Triangle.

name: Mauritius
description: Mauritius offers a surprising depth of diving beyond its beach reputation: an atmospheric WWII wreck, granite cathedral caves, and year-round warm water on the leeward west coast.

name: Réunion Island
description: A volcanic island with biodiverse reef systems regularly visited by humpback whales on their southern migration route, with unique coral diversity shaped by cold upwellings.

name: Kaş
description: A relaxed Lycian coast town where ancient history meets clear water: Bronze Age amphoras still visible on the seabed, dramatic canyon dive sites, and caves that cut through limestone cliffs into open sea.

name: Santorini
description: Below the famous caldera cliffs lies a unique volcanic underwater landscape with hot springs, pumice formations, and endemic species shaped by millennia of eruption cycles.

name: Lanzarote
description: Lanzarote hosts the world's first underwater sculpture museum and benefits from Atlantic upwellings that bring unexpected diversity to volcanic reef walls including angel sharks and rays year-round.

name: Okinawa
description: Japan's subtropical diving frontier with clear warm water on dramatic coral drop-offs and seasonal hammerhead schools that draw advanced divers to the outer reefs from spring through autumn.

name: Vava'u, Tonga
description: Tonga's Vava'u group is one of the only places on earth where humpback whales actively seek out encounters with in-water observers, turning July through October into something genuinely unlike anywhere else.

name: Rarotonga, Cook Islands
description: Remote Cook Islands diving where pristine outer reef walls drop steeply into deep blue water and passes fill seasonally with manta rays and grey reef sharks hunting in the current.

name: Turks and Caicos Islands
description: The wall dives here rank among the Caribbean's finest: the Providenciales reef edge drops vertically from 15 m to beyond 2000 m with crystalline 30 m visibility and regular pelagic flyovers.

name: Dominica
description: The Nature Isle of the Caribbean with the most dramatic underwater landscape in the Lesser Antilles: volcanic bubbles, black sand slopes, and the world's only resident sperm whale population accessible to divers.

name: Barbados
description: Barbados punches above its size with 6 accessible wrecks in a single bay and year-round sea turtle encounters on calm leeward reefs welcoming divers of every level.

name: Sea of Cortez
description: Jacques Cousteau called it the world's aquarium: the Sea of Cortez holds more marine species than anywhere else on earth and the only living coral reef in the entire Gulf of California.

name: Phuket
description: Thailand's dive capital with a fully protected marine park 20 minutes offshore and a year-round liveaboard departure point for the Andaman Sea.

name: Bali
description: Bali offers startling variety within one island: drift walls in the west, a WWII wreck in the north, and seasonal ocean sunfish encounters in the strait to the east.

name: Gili Islands
description: 3 tiny car-free islands off Lombok ringed by turtle-dense reefs and some of the friendliest shark dives in the region.

name: Lembeh Strait
description: The world capital of muck diving: a narrow strait in North Sulawesi where volcanic black sand conceals a rotating cast of rare and bizarre macro life found nowhere else in such density.

name: Espiritu Santo
description: Espiritu Santo offers world-class wreck diving alongside pristine coral reefs, with the best conditions from May through October when visibility regularly exceeds 30 meters.

name: Bikini Atoll
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site, Bikini Atoll hosts an extraordinary fleet of WW2 warships transformed into artificial reefs, best dived from March through May when seas are calmest.

name: Tuvalu
description: Tuvalu's remote atolls shelter pristine coral gardens and abundant fish life rarely touched by tourism, with April through November offering the clearest conditions.

name: New Caledonia
description: New Caledonia's double barrier reef — the world's second largest — encloses spectacular lagoons and dramatic wall dives, with peak conditions from August through November.

name: Madang
description: Madang is PNG's macro diving capital, offering extraordinary critter-rich muck sites alongside healthy coral gardens, with year-round warm water and best visibility from May through October.

name: Tufi
description: Tufi's dramatic fjord coastline conceals WW2 aircraft wrecks and some of PNG's most pristine coral walls, best explored from May through November when the southeast trade winds bring clear water.

name: Baa Atoll
description: Baa Atoll is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve famed for Hanifaru Bay, where hundreds of manta rays and whale sharks gather to feed during the southwest monsoon from June to November.

name: South Male Atoll
description: South Male Atoll offers dramatic channel dives at Guraidhoo Corner and Embudhoo Canyon, with strong currents delivering pelagic encounters including grey reef sharks, eagle rays, and hammerheads year-round.

name: Lhaviyani Atoll
description: Lhaviyani Atoll is celebrated for the Shipyard dive site and Fushivaru Thila, offering exceptional coral gardens, large nurse sharks, and strong current thilas teeming with reef fish from November through April.

name: Aldabra Atoll
description: Aldabra Atoll is one of the world's most remote UNESCO World Heritage Sites, offering pristine untouched reefs, schools of hammerhead sharks, giant manta rays, and enormous populations of green turtles best visited April through October.

name: Rodrigues Island
description: Rodrigues Island, a small volcanic island 650 km east of Mauritius, boasts a vast pristine lagoon and unspoiled coral ecosystems with exceptional visibility and encounters with spinner dolphins and reef sharks, best from October through April.

name: Neil Island (Andaman Islands)
description: Neil Island in the Andaman archipelago offers relaxed coral gardens and drift dives with excellent macro life, reef sharks, and turtles in warm, clear waters best explored from October through May.

name: Coron
description: Coron Bay harbors one of the world's greatest wreck diving collections — a fleet of Japanese ships sunk in a 1944 US air raid — best dived from October through May when visibility peaks and seas are calm.

name: Apo Island
description: Apo Island's community-managed marine sanctuary off Negros Oriental is a textbook conservation success story, with thriving reef fish populations and reliable green sea turtle encounters best enjoyed from March through June.

name: Banda Sea
description: Indonesia's remote Banda Sea rewards liveaboard expeditions with pristine walls, schooling hammerheads, and volcanic topography that few divers ever witness — the October to April dry season brings the best visibility and pelagic action.

name: Alor
description: Alor's fierce upwelling currents carry cold nutrient-rich water that supports an extraordinary diversity of rare macro critters, pygmy seahorses, and large pelagics — dive from April through November for optimal conditions.

name: Ambon Bay
description: Ambon Bay is the world capital of rare critter diving, where the muck and rubble slopes conceal walking sharks, Rhinopias scorpionfish, and mimic octopus — year-round diving is possible with the best macro encounters from September through March.

name: Con Dao
description: Con Dao National Park's protected waters off southern Vietnam shelter dugong, hawksbill turtles, and intact coral gardens rarely visited by divers — February through June delivers the calmest seas and clearest water.

name: Phu Quoc
description: Phu Quoc's southern islands and the An Thoi archipelago offer gentle coral garden diving with soft corals, nudibranchs, and schooling fish — the November through April dry season brings the best conditions in the Gulf of Thailand.

name: Corn Islands
description: Little Corn Island delivers pristine, crowd-free Caribbean reef diving year-round, with the dry season (March through June) bringing peak visibility and the calmest seas. Nurse sharks, eagle rays, and healthy hard coral gardens thrive in near-complete isolation from mass tourism.

name: Azores — Faial and Pico
description: The mid-Atlantic seamounts off Faial and Pico are a world-class big-animal destination, with Princess Alice Bank drawing mobula rays, blue sharks, mako sharks and oceanic whitetips from June through October. Volcanic geology, sperm whale encounters and rich cold-water reefs round out one of Europe's most adventurous dive circuits.

name: Bazaruto Archipelago
description: Bazaruto Archipelago National Park shelters one of the last viable dugong populations in the western Indian Ocean alongside whale sharks, manta rays, and pristine coral reefs that see only a handful of divers each year — October through February brings whale sharks and the calmest channel crossings.

name: Mafia Island
description: Mafia Island Marine Park in Tanzania's Chole Bay protects a mosaic of coral gardens, seagrass beds, and offshore reefs where whale sharks aggregate October through February and nesting green and hawksbill turtles return year after year — the dry season from June to October brings exceptional visibility.

name: Grand Cayman
description: Grand Cayman pairs dramatic wall diving along the North Wall with intimate cave systems and one of the Caribbean's most celebrated wreck dives. Warm, clear water and reliable visibility make it a year-round destination, with the calmest seas from November through April.

name: Cayman Brac
description: Cayman Brac is a quieter sister island with a stellar Soviet-era warship wreck, vibrant wall diving, and accessible reef systems that reward macro photographers. Best conditions run from November through April when northeast trade winds ease and seas flatten.

name: Tobago
description: Tobago's southwest and central coasts deliver some of the Caribbean's most underrated reef diving — massive brain corals, sponge forests, and twin rock pinnacles all within a short boat ride of Crown Point. January through May brings settled seas and visibility regularly exceeding 25 metres.

name: Grenada
description: Grenada combines the world-famous Bianca C wreck with a surprisingly varied topside of shallow sculpture reefs, volcanic drop-offs, and healthy soft coral gardens — best visited January through May when trade winds keep conditions calm and visibility crisp.

name: Guadeloupe
description: Guadeloupe's Cousteau Reserve around Pigeon Island protects one of the Caribbean's best-preserved reef systems, while Basse-Terre's volcanic walls drop into clear Atlantic blue — best dived from December through April when trade winds bring settled seas and 30-metre-plus visibility.

name: Martinique
description: Martinique pairs an iconic open-water pinnacle at Diamond Rock with sheltered lagoon dives at Anses d'Arlet, where hawksbill turtles graze year-round — the dry season window of December to May brings the clearest water and calmest surface conditions.

name: Marseille Calanques
description: The Calanques National Park offers some of the Mediterranean's most dramatic diving — limestone cliffs plunging into gin-clear water, the Cassidaigne seamount drawing huge barracuda and dentex, and cathedral-like caves explored since Jacques Cousteau's era. May through October delivers the best visibility and warm surface temperatures.

name: Ustica Island
description: Italy's first marine reserve, Ustica sits 60 km north of Palermo and protects waters so rich that giant grouper cruise openly between divers and posidonia meadows stretch to 40 m depth. June through September is prime season with warm water and visibility regularly exceeding 30 m.

name: Rhodes
description: Rhodes offers year-round diving with peak visibility from June through October, when warm 26 C waters reveal ancient amphora fields, dense sponge gardens, and moray-laced reef walls stretching to 40 m. The island's protected eastern bays shelter wrecks while the west coast delivers dramatic drop-offs swept by mild Aegean currents.

name: Crete
description: Greece's largest island rewards divers with cave systems, sea fan gardens, and imposing wall dives, best explored between May and November when visibility regularly exceeds 30 m and water temperatures climb to 25 C. Chania's northwest coast hides the spectacular Elephant Cave, while the northern shelf near Heraklion hosts rare gorgonian forests at manageable depths.

name: Menorca
description: The least developed of the Balearic Islands rewards divers with pristine posidonia meadows, dramatic limestone caves, and vibrant red gorgonian fans; June through October delivers warm, clear water and calm seas across the island's protected marine reserves.

name: Tremiti Islands
description: Italy's only offshore Adriatic archipelago and a protected marine reserve, the Tremiti group draws divers with cathedral sea caves, sheer walls draped in gorgonians and sponges, and dense aggregations of large sea breams and brown grouper; the clearest water and most stable conditions run from May through September.

name: Gladden Spit
description: Gladden Spit is one of the world's most dramatic large-pelagic encounters — whale sharks converge on the spawning aggregations of cubera and mutton snapper during full moons from March through June, creating a feeding spectacle unlike anywhere else in the Caribbean. Outside spawning season the offshore wall drops into spectacular blue water with healthy soft coral gardens and consistent shark action.

name: Long Island
description: Long Island anchors its diving reputation on Dean's Blue Hole, the world's deepest known saltwater blue hole at 202 metres, a pilgrimage site for freedivers and a hauntingly beautiful dive for scuba divers down to recreational limits. The surrounding barrier reef walls and shallow coral gardens offer pristine conditions far from the crowds of Nassau.

name: Madeira
description: Madeira's volcanic underwater terrain delivers year-round diving with best visibility and warmest water from June through October; the Garajau Natural Reserve protects dense fish populations and endemic species within easy reach of Funchal.

name: Tenerife
description: Tenerife offers year-round diving in the Canary Islands with endemic species, angel shark encounters at El Puertito, and dramatic volcanic walls; water temperatures stay comfortable in all months and visibility peaks from July through November.

name: Trondheim Fjord
description: Norway's Trondheim Fjord and the Agdenes outer coast offer some of the world's most dramatic cold-water diving — crystal-clear water down to 30 m visibility in winter, walls blanketed in plumose anemones, resident wolf fish, and giant king crab. Best diving runs May through September when surface water warms slightly and visibility peaks, though adventurous drysuiters dive year-round.

name: St. Abbs Head
description: The St. Abbs and Eyemouth Voluntary Marine Reserve on the Scottish Borders coast is the UK's most celebrated cold-water dive destination — towering kelp forests, labyrinthine gullies, resident wolf fish and conger eels, and cuckoo wrasse in vivid breeding colours. Visibility frequently exceeds 15 m, and the reserve's no-take status has produced unusually abundant marine life. Best months are April through September.

name: Muscat
description: Oman's capital coastline delivers year-round subtropical diving anchored by whale sharks, schooling barracuda, and healthy coral gardens — October through May brings the clearest water and calmest seas.

name: Izu Peninsula
description: A cold-water macro paradise south of Tokyo, the Izu Peninsula hosts volcanic seabeds teeming with nudibranchs, frogfish, and blennies against dramatic drop-offs. Seasonal thermoclines bring nutrients that sustain one of Japan's richest invertebrate communities.

name: Maui
description: Maui's waters encompass an ancient volcanic caldera, spinner dolphin corridors, lava tube labyrinths, and legendary manta ray night encounters. Endemic Hawaiian species and gin-clear visibility make it one of the Pacific's most diverse island diving destinations.

name: Oahu
description: Oahu blends accessible wreck diving with vibrant reef ecosystems nurtured by warm Pacific currents. Artificial reefs like the YO-257 have transformed into thriving coral habitats, while volcanic lava formations shelter green sea turtles and reef sharks year-round.

name: Lord Howe Island
description: Lord Howe Island is a UNESCO World Heritage site in the Tasman Sea where subtropical and tropical marine zones converge, producing extraordinary biodiversity found nowhere else on Earth. The island's remote pinnacles, dramatic walls, and endemic species including the Lord Howe double-header wrasse make it one of the Southern Hemisphere's most extraordinary dive destinations.

name: Manado
description: Manado Bay and its satellite islands deliver some of Indonesia's most diverse reef diving, from sheer volcanic walls draped in soft coral to rubble-strewn macro hotspots. Pygmy seahorses, nudibranchs, and schooling pelagics share the same reefs, making every dive multilayered.

name: Halmahera
description: Remote and rarely dived, Halmahera sits at the heart of the Coral Triangle and harbours extraordinary biodiversity including walking epaulette sharks, pygmy seahorses, and manta rays funnelling through the island's narrow passages. Its isolation has kept reefs in near-pristine condition.

name: Mactan Island
description: A flat coral island just minutes from Cebu City by bridge, Mactan punches above its weight with turtle-rich channels, colourful macro reefs, and one of the Philippines' most dramatic submerged caves — all reachable on a short boat ride.

name: Pigeon Island
description: A national park marine sanctuary off Trincomalee's northeast coast, Pigeon Island is Sri Lanka's premier reef diving destination, famous for its resident blacktip reef shark population and some of the island nation's healthiest coral gardens.

name: Batticaloa
description: A rarely dived stretch of Sri Lanka's southeast coast, Batticaloa combines pristine fringing reefs with one of the world's most remarkable wreck dives — the HMS Hermes, the largest diveable aircraft carrier wreck on the planet — and seasonal sperm whale encounters offshore.

name: Apia, Samoa
description: Samoa's reefs around Apia and the south-eastern Aleipata Islands hold some of the least-dived coral in the central Pacific, with giant clam gardens, dense surgeonfish aggregations, and a protected marine reserve right off the capital.

name: Mariana Islands (Guam & Saipan)
description: Guam and Saipan anchor the Mariana chain with a rare mix of WWII wrecks, dramatic cavern dives, and open-ocean pelagic action where the Philippine Sea meets some of the deepest water on earth.

name: Gran Canaria
description: Gran Canaria's volcanic seabed delivers year-round diving through lava tunnels, deep canyons, and an extraordinary artificial reef — with endemic species, angel sharks, and resident barracuda schools making this Canarian island a genuine Atlantic diving destination.

name: Fuerteventura
description: Fuerteventura sits just 100 kilometres off the African coast where Atlantic upwellings fuel a productive marine ecosystem — manta ray aggregations, pelagic encounters, wreck diving, and exceptional macro life reward divers who look beyond the island's famous beaches.

name: Andros
description: Andros is the largest island in the Bahamas and home to the third longest barrier reef in the world, stretching over 190 km along its eastern shore. It is legendary for its blue holes — both inland freshwater caves and ocean-connected shafts — and for reliable shark encounters along its deep-water channels.

name: Easter Island
description: Easter Island (Rapa Nui) sits in the southeastern Pacific more than 3,500 km from the nearest continent, making it one of the most isolated dive destinations on Earth. Its volcanic geology, crystal-clear waters, and high endemism — including species found nowhere else — attract divers seeking truly untouched reefs, while submerged moai statues and a historic wreck add a layer of cultural mystique.

name: Diani / Shimoni
description: Kenya's southern coast hides one of East Africa's most biodiverse marine parks in Kisite-Mpunguti, where whale sharks patrol October to February and humpback whales sing through the channel from June to September, all above pristine hard coral gardens that see a fraction of the tourist pressure of the north.

name: Zanzibar Island
description: Zanzibar's main island is ringed by dramatically varied diving — from the oceanic seamount of Leven Bank where great hammerheads and silky sharks circle in blue water, to the reef sharks of Pange Sandbank and the colourful hard coral gardens just minutes from historic Stone Town.

name: Djerba
description: Tunisia's largest island sits at the edge of the Gulf of Gabès, offering a rarely-visited Mediterranean dive scene of ancient wrecks, sponge-draped reefs, and prolific cephalopods. Warm, calm conditions and excellent visibility make it one of North Africa's most accessible dive destinations.

name: Algarve
description: Portugal's southern coast combines dramatic limestone cliffs, cold Atlantic upwelling, and a rich diversity of Atlantic species that rarely appear in warmer European waters. From the protected granite outcrops of the Berlengas reserve to the deep seamounts off Aljezur and the cargo wrecks near Sagres, the Algarve rewards divers who embrace temperate conditions.

name: Bermuda
description: The wreck capital of the Atlantic, Bermuda's shallow subtropical platform holds over 300 documented shipwrecks spanning five centuries, from Spanish galleons to World War II vessels, all draped in hard corals and patrolled by schooling fish. Mid-Atlantic isolation has produced a distinctive reef community of green moray eels, spiny lobster, and vast sergeant major aggregations surrounded by some of the clearest waters in the western Atlantic.

name: Saint Helena Island
description: One of the most remote islands on Earth, Saint Helena rises from the South Atlantic in pristine isolation, its underwater cliffs and seamounts drawing whale sharks, devil rays, and vast schools of yellowfin tuna through waters virtually untouched by dive tourism. The island's remoteness has preserved reef ecosystems that larger, more accessible destinations lost decades ago.

name: Marsa Alam
description: Marsa Alam anchors Egypt's southern Red Sea coast where remote reefs, resident dugongs, and oceanic predators converge in one of the least-crowded stretches of the Egyptian coastline. Abu Dabbab Bay shelters dugongs year-round, Elphinstone Reef ranks among the world's premier shark dives, and Marsa Mubarak rewards patient divers with turtle encounters on every visit.

name: Fury Shoals
description: Fury Shoals is a remote reef labyrinth 200 km south of Marsa Alam accessible only by liveaboard, rewarding those who make the journey with world-class dolphin encounters, pristine coral gardens, and reliable oceanic whitetip sightings. Shaab Sataya is globally renowned as one of the finest spinner dolphin dive sites on the planet.

name: US Virgin Islands
description: Three distinct islands — St. John, St. Croix, and St. Thomas — offer a spectrum of Caribbean diving from protected national park reefs to dramatic deep canyon walls draped in black coral.

name: Anguilla
description: Anguilla's flat coral limestone terrain drops into spectacularly clear water — frequently exceeding 30 metres of visibility — making it one of the Caribbean's best-kept diving secrets. Sandy Island's pristine patch reef, the dramatic Prickly Pear walls, the MV Ida Maria wreck and the remote Dog Island seamount give divers pristine encounters with nurse sharks, tarpon, and barracuda far from the crowds.

name: Saint Kitts and Nevis
description: Rising from the sea on a young volcanic arc, Saint Kitts and Nevis pair lush rainforest slopes with outstanding diving on black-coral walls, productive turtle-frequented shallow reefs and the dramatic River Taw wreck — a 225-foot tug that sits upright and intact in 30 metres of crystal-clear Caribbean water.

name: Antigua and Barbuda
description: Antigua and Barbuda pairs 365 white-sand beaches with a rich underwater heritage of coral-encrusted colonial wrecks, dramatic sea caves, and a vibrant barrier reef sheltering nurse sharks and hawksbill turtles. Warm Caribbean waters averaging 27–29°C and excellent year-round visibility make these twin islands one of the Eastern Caribbean's most rewarding dive destinations.

name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
description: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines stretches across 32 islands and cays, offering everything from the pristine Tobago Cays Marine Park — one of the Caribbean's last true wilderness reefs — to deep-water pelagic encounters with manta rays and migrating humpback whales off Bequia. The diversity of habitats, from volcanic black-sand slopes to shallow seagrass meadows, supports exceptional biodiversity year-round.

name: Tioman Island
description: One of the world's most beautiful islands, Tioman sits off Pahang state in the South China Sea, its marine park reefs sheltering blacktip reef sharks, green turtles, and vast schools of pelagics amid pristine hard and soft coral gardens.

name: Redang Island
description: A jewel of Terengganu's marine park, Redang Island is famed for its crystal-clear waters, enormous bumphead parrotfish schools, and seasonal whale shark encounters from June through August.

name: Lankayan Island
description: A remote coral island in the Sulu Sea off Sabah's northeast coast, Lankayan is one of Malaysia's finest whale shark destinations with year-round sightings over pristine sandy-bottom reefs and a handful of atmospheric wrecks.

name: Kapalai Island
description: Perched on a submerged sandbar between Sipadan and Mabul, Kapalai is a stilted-resort muck-diving paradise where shallow rubble slopes and sea-grass beds conceal an astonishing density of bizarre and cryptic critters.

name: Kerama Islands
description: The Kerama Islands sit 30 kilometres west of Naha, Okinawa, sheltered within a national park renowned for coral density rivalling the Great Barrier Reef. Humpback whales calve in these waters January through March, while manta rays and sea turtles are resident year-round.

name: Ishigaki Island
description: Ishigaki Island anchors the Yaeyama archipelago at the southwestern tip of Japan, earning its reputation as the manta ray capital of the world thanks to a large resident population that aggregates daily at predictable cleaning stations. Beyond mantas, the island offers blue holes, schooling pelagics, and pristine hard coral gardens in some of the clearest water in East Asia.

name: Portofino
description: Portofino Marine Protected Area on the Ligurian Riviera shelters crystal-clear cold Mediterranean waters rich with posidonia meadows, conger eels, and the iconic Cristo degli Abissi bronze statue resting at 17 metres.

name: Aeolian Islands
description: This volcanic archipelago north of Sicily offers dramatic underwater geology — black sand seabeds, volcanic walls, and posidonia meadows — alongside rich reef fish populations and rare Roman amphorae scattered along Basiluzzo's rocky ledges.

name: Isla Mujeres
description: Isla Mujeres sits at the tip of the Yucatán Peninsula where Caribbean currents funnel nutrient-rich upwellings past pristine reefs, making it the world capital of whale shark aggregations from June through September. The island pairs accessible reef diving with the surreal MUSA underwater sculpture museum and strong year-round populations of sea turtles and eagle rays.

name: Banco Chinchorro
description: Banco Chinchorro is Mexico's largest coral atoll and one of the most remote dive destinations in the Caribbean, lying 50 km off the southern Yucatán coast. Its shallow lagoon harbours one of the only places on Earth where divers can encounter American saltwater crocodiles face to face, while the outer walls and historic wrecks remain virtually untouched.

name: Tayrona National Park
description: Colombia's crown jewel of Caribbean conservation, Tayrona sits where the Sierra Nevada mountains plunge directly into the sea, creating a dramatic backdrop for reef systems sheltered within a protected national park. Nurse sharks patrol sandy channels, spotted eagle rays cruise coral gardens, and sea turtles nest on beaches framed by ancient indigenous culture.

name: Rosario Islands
description: An archipelago of 27 coral islands 35 km southwest of Cartagena, the Islas del Rosario sits within a national park where coral walls descend past giant barrel sponges, black coral forests, and encrusted wrecks. Seahorses cling to sea fans in shallow gardens while spotted morays and lobsters inhabit deeper ledges, making this the most accessible Caribbean wall diving in Colombia.

name: SS Yongala Wreck
description: One of the world's greatest wreck dives, the SS Yongala sank in 1911 during a cyclone and now rests intact at 15 to 28 metres, blanketed in coral and teeming with an extraordinary density of marine life. Bull sharks, giant gropers, marble rays, and sea snakes patrol the vessel year-round in the warm waters off Townsville.

name: Osprey Reef
description: A remote coral atoll rising from the deep Coral Sea some 350 kilometres from Cairns, Osprey Reef delivers world-class shark encounters, dramatic wall diving, and oceanic pelagic action that liveaboard divers travel the globe to experience. The reef's isolation keeps it pristine, with visibility often exceeding 50 metres in the crystalline open-ocean water.

name: Kadavu Island
description: Kadavu is Fiji's fourth largest island and home to the Great Astrolabe Reef, the world's fourth largest barrier reef system. Year-round manta ray encounters, pelagic action on the passes, and wall diving with napoleon wrasse and hammerheads make this one of the Pacific's most rewarding remote destinations.

name: Yasawa Islands
description: The Yasawa Islands stretch 90 kilometres through northern Fiji as a remote chain of volcanic islands ringed by pristine, largely uncrowded reefs. Seasonal manta ray aggregations in Drawaqa Passage, playful spinner dolphins, and crystalline visibility above 30 metres draw divers seeking classic Fijian colour without the crowds.

name: Puerto Rico
description: Puerto Rico offers world-class diving across remote offshore reserves and dramatic deepwater walls, with exceptional water clarity and thriving populations of Caribbean megafauna including sharks, rays, and sea turtles.

name: Sint Maarten / Saint-Martin
description: This dual-nation island in the northeastern Caribbean combines a historic shipwreck, shallow stingray flats, and labyrinthine reef formations, offering accessible diving in warm, clear waters year-round.

name: North Carolina
description: North Carolina's Outer Banks coast earned its nickname the Graveyard of the Atlantic for good reason — more than 5,000 ships have sunk in these waters over four centuries. WWII wrecks lie in remarkable condition just offshore, drawing divers who want history and big wildlife in equal measure.

name: British Columbia
description: British Columbia's cold, nutrient-rich Pacific waters host some of the world's densest concentrations of giant Pacific octopus alongside wolf eels, Steller sea lions, and orcas. From the sheltered passages of the Inside Passage to the exposed rocky reefs of Race Rocks, BC delivers a cold-water spectacle unmatched on the continent.

name: Ireland
description: Ireland's Atlantic coastline delivers surprisingly rich cold-water diving, from Europe's first marine nature reserve at Lough Hyne to basking shark corridors off Rathlin Island and dramatic sea cave systems carved into the Carboniferous limestone of County Clare.

name: Wales (Pembrokeshire)
description: Pembrokeshire's Pembrokeshire Coast National Park hides some of Britain's most accessible cold-water diving — grey seal nurseries on Skomer Island, blue shark summer aggregations at The Smalls, and bottlenose dolphin encounters in Cardigan Bay's sheltered waters.

name: Lofoten Islands
description: The Lofoten Islands rise dramatically from the Arctic Sea above the Arctic Circle, offering world class cold water diving in crystalline visibility among wolf fish, king crab, and towering kelp forests. Sperm whales and orcas visit seasonally, making this one of the most extraordinary diving destinations in the northern hemisphere.

name: Faroe Islands
description: The Faroe Islands sit in the North Atlantic between Iceland and Norway, a volcanic archipelago of dramatic underwater cliffs, sea caves, and rich cold water marine life including grey seals, wolffish, and dense populations of nudibranchs. Powerful Atlantic currents fuel exceptional biodiversity in these remote, rarely dived waters.

name: False Bay
description: False Bay is one of South Africa's most dramatic dive destinations, where cold nutrient-rich Atlantic upwellings meet a rugged coastline to support an extraordinary ecosystem anchored by great white sharks, Cape fur seals, and rare subtropical corals.

name: Quirimbas Archipelago
description: The Quirimbas Archipelago stretches across 250 kilometres of remote northern Mozambique coastline, offering some of the most pristine and rarely dived reefs in the Indian Ocean, with whale sharks, manta rays, and untouched coral systems that see only a handful of divers each year.

name: Corsica
description: France's most spectacular Mediterranean diving destination, Corsica blends UNESCO-listed Scandola Marine Reserve with wild rocky coastlines where dusky grouper, barracuda, and red coral thrive in crystalline waters.

name: Ibiza and Formentera
description: The western Mediterranean's hidden dive jewel, Ibiza and Formentera sit above UNESCO-protected Posidonia oceanica meadows — ancient seagrass forests sheltering barracuda, dentex, and sea bass in waters of extraordinary clarity.

name: Mirissa
description: Mirissa sits on Sri Lanka's southwestern tip and hosts the highest density blue whale encounters on earth from January to April, alongside whale sharks, hammerhead sharks, and vibrant coral reefs accessible to all levels.

name: Mayotte
description: Mayotte is a French overseas territory nestled between Madagascar and Mozambique, encircled by one of the world's largest and most intact coral lagoons, with whale sharks, humpback whales, and resident manta rays year-round.

name: Rabaul
description: Rabaul sits inside a volcanic caldera on the northeastern tip of New Britain, where a sheltered harbor holds one of the Pacific's most dramatic collections of WWII Japanese shipwrecks. The combination of vivid coral growth on intact wartime cargo and the backdrop of smoking volcanoes makes this a singular destination for wreck divers.

name: Pohnpei
description: Pohnpei is a high volcanic island ringed by one of the largest lagoons in Micronesia, offering a rare mix of pristine outer reef passages, offshore seamount pinnacles, and the famous manta aggregation sites at Ant Atoll. Far less visited than Chuuk, it rewards divers who seek untouched reef ecosystems and reliable pelagic encounters.

name: Isabela Island
description: The largest island in the Galapagos archipelago, Isabela offers some of the most dramatic underwater encounters in the world — from whale sharks at Tortuga Negra Bay to Mola mola at Vicente Roca Point and marine iguanas feeding on algae at Cape Marshall.

name: Fernandina Island
description: The most volcanically active and ecologically pristine island in the Galapagos, Fernandina's underwater world teems with marine iguanas, Galapagos penguins, sea lions, and open-ocean giants like Mola mola drifting in the cold nutrient-rich currents.

name: Bodrum
description: Bodrum sits on the turquoise Turkish Aegean where ancient trade routes have left a seafloor scattered with amphora-laden wrecks and reef systems rich with Mediterranean marine life. Karaada's geothermal vents and the region's rocky reefs draw dentex, grouper, and octopus year-round.

name: Marmaris
description: Marmaris anchors the Turkish Riviera where pine-forested peninsulas drop into clear blue water, sheltering superb dive sites around Cleopatra Island, Çiftlik Bay, and the mirror-calm Aquarium Bay. The region's posidonia meadows, sponge gardens, and resident sea turtles make it one of the Aegean's most accessible and ecologically rich dive destinations.

name: María la Gorda
description: Perched at Cuba's remote western tip, María la Gorda sits where the Gulf of Mexico meets the Caribbean — delivering some of the island's richest wall diving, black coral gardens, and a legendary cathedral cavern draped in sponges.

name: Bay of Pigs
description: Playa Girón and the Bay of Pigs combine Cold War history with crystalline cenotes and a pristine marine reserve — wall dives tumble from shallow coral terraces to open-water abyss while hawksbill turtles and vast barracuda schools patrol the blue.

name: Abrolhos Banks
description: A remote reef archipelago off the coast of Bahia, the Abrolhos Banks host the highest density of humpback whales in the South Atlantic from June to November, alongside unique mushroom-shaped chapeirão coral formations found nowhere else on Earth.

name: Paraty
description: Nestled along the Rio de Janeiro coast, Paraty offers pristine reef diving around protected islands and a naval research reserve, where untouched ecosystems harbor sea turtles, moray eels, and abundant macro life in gin-clear waters.

name: Montenegro
description: Montenegro's Adriatic coastline packs dramatic limestone karst, sea caves, and a surprisingly rich temperate reef ecosystem into a compact stretch of coast — cave diving at Sveti Nikola, seahorse colonies at Žanjice Bay, and blue-light refraction caves near Budva make it one of Europe's most underrated dive destinations.

name: Albania
description: Albania's protected Riviera coastline is one of the Mediterranean's last undiscovered dive frontiers — the Karaburun Peninsula marine reserve shelters large grouper and loggerhead turtles, while Cold War military wrecks at Sazan Island and Porto Palermo's submarine tunnel offer historically unique artifact diving found nowhere else in Europe.

name: Netrani Island
description: Netrani Island — known as the Pigeon Island of India — rises from the Arabian Sea off Karnataka with dramatically steep underwater walls and one of the most reliable whale shark aggregations in Asia, drawing divers from October through May for encounters with the ocean's largest fish alongside schooling dogtooth tuna and resident sea turtles.

name: Addu Atoll
description: Addu Atoll, the southernmost and most geographically isolated of the Maldivian atolls, combines the best Maldivian wreck diving — the British Loyalty tanker sunk in 1946 — with pristine manta ray channels and resident shark populations that benefit from light visitor numbers compared to the heavily dived northern atolls.

name: Dubai
description: Dubai's artificial reef programme has transformed decommissioned vessels and purpose-sunk structures into thriving hard coral communities in the warm Gulf shallows, offering accessible wreck diving minutes from the world's busiest airport.

name: Abu Dhabi
description: Abu Dhabi's sheltered Gulf waters conceal the SS Mercury wreck and a string of quiet reef structures that draw hawksbill turtles, rays, and reef sharks, making it a rewarding urban escape for divers based in the UAE capital.

name: Bahrain
description: Bahrain sits at the heart of the Arabian Gulf's ancient pearl-diving culture and now offers divers coral gardens, dugong encounters at Hawar Islands, and the purpose-sunk Lost Paradise of Dilmun artificial reef, all in gin-clear winter waters.

name: Musandam Peninsula
description: Oman's dramatic exclave jutting into the Strait of Hormuz, where towering limestone fjords (khors) plunge into nutrient-rich waters teeming with endemic Arabian Gulf fish, giant grouper, and seasonal whale sharks and hammerheads.

name: Kalpitiya
description: Northwestern Sri Lanka's wild coast hosts the country's largest coral reef system and some of the Indian Ocean's most reliable whale shark aggregations and spinner dolphin superpods, best experienced from January through April.

name: Bayahibe
description: Bayahibe sits on the southeast coast of the Dominican Republic, framed by protected cays and a marine reserve that shelters some of the most biodiverse coral gardens in the Greater Antilles. Wrecks, caves, and pristine fringing reefs draw divers from La Caleta to the outer walls of Catalinita.

name: Haiti
description: Haiti's coastline holds some of the least dived reefs in the Caribbean, where decades of low boat traffic have allowed coral gardens, hawksbill turtles, and nurse sharks to recover in near-pristine conditions. From the offshore cays of Ile à Vache to the brain coral shoals of Les Arcadins marine reserve, the country rewards adventurous divers willing to go off the beaten path.

name: Kosrae
description: Kosrae is FSM's smallest and most remote main island, encircled by pristine mangrove-fringed fringing reefs that scientists consistently rank among the healthiest coral ecosystems in all of Micronesia. Its isolation has kept fishing pressure low and corals largely untouched, rewarding divers with walls, blue holes, and shark-dense outer passes that feel entirely undiscovered.

name: Efate
description: Efate is Vanuatu's capital island and home to Port Vila, offering accessible wreck diving, vibrant macro reefs, and exceptional night dives all reachable within minutes of the harbour. The island's volcanic geology creates dramatic underwater formations that shelter an extraordinary diversity of nudibranchs, frogfish, and crustaceans alongside the island's celebrated WWII wrecks.

name: Kauai
description: Hawaii's oldest and most geologically dramatic island, Kauai offers pristine reef diving along the Na Pali Coast, rare access to the protected waters off Niihau, and lava tube systems teeming with endemic Hawaiian species and spinner dolphins.

name: Lanai
description: Lanai's sheltered leeward coast offers some of Hawaii's clearest water and most spectacular lava tube cathedral formations, with resident spinner dolphins, giant moray eels, and exceptional visibility year-round.

name: Amami Islands
description: The Amami Islands sit in subtropical Kagoshima prefecture, draped in dense coral gardens and visited seasonally by whale sharks and manta rays. Their endemic species richness and near-pristine reef systems make them one of Japan's most ecologically significant diving destinations.

name: Miyako Islands
description: The Miyako Islands lie in Okinawa prefecture between the main Okinawa island and the Yaeyama group, sheltering Yabiji — Japan's largest coral reef — alongside crystalline blue caves and reliable manta ray cleaning stations. Exceptional water clarity and consistent currents draw pelagic species throughout the warmer months.

name: Kenting National Park
description: Taiwan's southernmost diving destination, warmed by the Kuroshio Current and sheltered by Kenting National Park. Reef systems here support surprising biodiversity from triggerfish and fusiliers to moray eels and lionfish.

name: Little Liuqiu Island
description: A small coral island off Pingtung County famous for the highest density of wild green sea turtles in Taiwan. Fringing reefs and sea caves ring the island, delivering turtle encounters on virtually every dive.

name: Sainte-Marie Island
description: Île Sainte-Marie sits off Madagascar's northeast coast and doubles as a humpback whale nursery every July to September. Its reefs remain largely unexplored, and the bay shelters intact 17th-century pirate ship wrecks beneath clear tropical water.

name: Chumbe Island Coral Park
description: Chumbe Island Coral Park is the first privately managed marine sanctuary in Africa, protecting over 350 coral species in the Zanzibar Channel. Strict no-take zoning since 1994 means the reef ecosystem — crowned by giant humphead parrotfish and patrolling eagle rays — has rebounded to exceptional density.

name: Milos
description: Milos is a volcanic Cyclades island renowned for its dramatic underwater geology — sea caves carved by wave and heat, basalt columns, and thermal vents that hint at the island's fiery origins. The marine environment is rich with posidonia meadows, colourful sponges, and a surprising abundance of cephalopods and reef fish.

name: Corfu
description: Corfu sits in the Ionian Sea where Atlantic-influenced currents bring cooler, nutrient-rich water that supports a distinct marine community quite unlike the Aegean. Rocky drop-offs draped in gorgonians, posidonia meadows sheltering seahorses, and seasonal visits from loggerhead turtles make it one of Greece's most rewarding Ionian dive destinations.

name: Istria
description: Croatia's northwestern peninsula hosts the northern Adriatic's most celebrated wrecks alongside dolphin-patrolled marine parks — the Baron Gautsch passenger ship and a WWII B-17 bomber draw history-minded divers, while the Brijuni Islands national park offers crystalline water and Mediterranean biodiversity.

name: Huatulco
description: Huatulco sits on Oaxaca's Pacific coast, sheltering nine bays within a biosphere reserve where hawksbill turtles nest, whale sharks cruise seasonally, and Pacific spotted dolphins ride the bow waves of dive boats.

name: Loreto
description: Loreto anchors the central Sea of Cortez — Jacques Cousteau's "aquarium of the world" — where Coronado Island sea lions, schooling hammerheads around Carmen Island, and manta rays gliding over Danzante's walls reward divers year-round.

name: La Gomera
description: La Gomera is the least developed of the Canary Islands, ringed by dramatic underwater volcanic cliffs and basalt formations that plunge into deep Atlantic water. Angel sharks patrol sandy channels, barracuda school around headlands, and pristine walls host colourful gorgonians and black coral.

name: Galicia
description: Galicia's rugged Atlantic coastline hides one of Europe's richest cold-water ecosystems, sheltered within the Illas Atlánticas National Park. Grey seals, giant octopus, dense nudibranch populations, and centuries-old shipwrecks make this northwest corner of Spain a genuine bucket-list destination for temperate-water divers.

name: Zakynthos
description: Zakynthos is a Greek Ionian island renowned for its UNESCO-protected loggerhead sea turtle (Caretta caretta) nesting beaches and spectacular sea caves. The island blends dramatic limestone coastline with warm, clear Ionian waters that shelter seahorses, moray eels, and a rich cast of Mediterranean reef life.

name: Dumaguete
description: Dumaguete, on the south coast of Negros Oriental, is one of the Philippines' premier muck diving destinations, with the volcanic black-sand slopes of Dauin Marine Sanctuary sheltering an astounding density of macro critters. The surrounding waters also hold dramatic reef walls and schooling pelagics at nearby Sumilon Island, making this an outstanding multi-style dive region.

name: Donsol
description: Donsol in Sorsogon province hosts the largest known aggregation of whale sharks on earth, drawing hundreds of the gentle giants from November through June. Beyond whale shark encounters, the surrounding waters of Ticao Island and Bulan offer manta rays, thresher sharks, and vibrant reef ecosystems.

name: Flores
description: Flores stretches between Bali and Komodo in eastern Indonesia, offering startlingly diverse diving from WWII wreck graveyards in Maumere Bay to pristine dugong meadows in Riung's 17 Islands Marine Park. The region sits within the Coral Triangle and remains far less visited than its famous neighbors, rewarding explorers with extraordinary biodiversity.

name: São Miguel
description: The largest island in the Azores archipelago sits atop one of the Atlantic's richest upwelling zones, drawing blue whales, sperm whales, manta rays, and blue sharks year-round to waters that shift between tropical warmth and cold Atlantic surge.

name: Boa Vista
description: The easternmost island of Cape Verde anchors one of the Atlantic's most underrated dive destinations, where whale sharks cruise Santa Monica Bay from February through May and loggerhead turtles nest on beaches surrounded by reef walls alive with rays and barracuda.

name: Pozzuoli / Baiae
description: Naples Bay cradles one of the ancient world's most extraordinary dive sites — the submerged Roman city of Baiae, whose thermal-volcanic subsidience carried imperial villas, mosaic floors, and marble statues beneath 3 to 15 metres of clear Tyrrhenian water. Divers and even snorkellers glide above intact column capitals and Emperor Claudius's nymphaeum, while the surrounding volcanic seabed harbours surprisingly rich Mediterranean marine life.

name: Almería
description: Cabo de Gata-Níjar Natural Park shelters the best-preserved marine reserve in the western Mediterranean, where the arid volcanic coastline of southeast Spain plunges into crystalline water that hosts seagrass prairies, pelagic aggregations, and a seahorse population dense enough to earn El Ancón the informal title of seahorse capital of the Mediterranean. The park's near-total absence of river runoff delivers visibility regularly exceeding 20 metres.

name: Fethiye / Göcek
description: A sheltered turquoise coastline of pine-clad islands and ancient Lycian ruins, where Ottoman shipwrecks lie draped in soft corals and calm bays harbour seahorses, moray eels, and nesting sea turtles year-round.

name: Antalya
description: The Turkish Riviera's ancient harbour city spills into crystal clear water, offering submerged Roman and Hellenistic ruins, Bronze Age shipwreck territory, and reef walls patrolled by loggerhead turtles and dense schools of barracuda.

name: Senegal
description: West Africa's diving gem, where the Cap-Vert Peninsula meets the Atlantic with dramatic reef walls, colonial-era wrecks, and year-round warm water teeming with Atlantic grouper, barracuda, and nurse sharks.

name: Bijagós Islands
description: A UNESCO Biosphere Reserve of 88 islands off Guinea-Bissau, where saltwater hippopotamuses wade among mangroves, critically endangered sawfish patrol shallow bays, and nesting sea turtles crowd pristine beaches above untouched coral gardens.

name: Inhambane
description: Southern Mozambique's Inhambane Bay is one of the Indian Ocean's most reliable year-round whale shark and manta ray destinations, drawing gentle giants to warm, plankton-rich waters that flow between the bay and the open Mozambique Channel.

name: Eilat
description: Israel's only Red Sea port hosts the world's northernmost coral reef system, a remarkably well-conserved ecosystem protected by strict Israeli marine park regulations and cooler, nutrient-upwelling waters that keep corals thriving at the very edge of their range.

name: Eyjafjörður
description: Iceland's longest fjord stretches 60 km inland from the Arctic coast, offering otherworldly cold-water diving through kelp forests, dramatic lava columns, and the hauntingly intact wreck of the Danish steamer Dettifoss. Arctic marine life thrives in the crystal-clear glacial meltwater, with wolf fish, Atlantic cod, and dense sea urchin beds carpeting the fjord floor.

name: Oban
description: Oban is Scotland's diving capital, a sheltered harbour town on the west coast that serves as gateway to some of the richest cold-water diving in Europe. Grey seal colonies, dense kelp forests, pollack shoals, and multiple historic wrecks make this a year-round destination for those willing to brave the chilly temperatures.

name: Gorgona Island
description: A former prison island turned national park off Colombia's Pacific coast, Gorgona delivers some of the most dramatic large-pelagic encounters in the Eastern Pacific — hammerhead schools, humpback whales, and whale sharks patrol its nutrient-rich waters from June through November.

name: Paracas
description: Driven by the cold, nutrient-dense Humboldt Current, Paracas offers a uniquely Peruvian diving experience where sea lions, Humboldt penguins, and hammerhead sharks share frigid Pacific waters alongside cold-water corals — earning the region its nickname as the Galapagos of Peru.

name: Naxos
description: The largest of the Cyclades islands, Naxos offers productive Aegean diving across Posidonia seagrass meadows, dramatic reefs, and the haunting silhouette of ancient submerged ruins. Strong seasonal currents attract barracuda, dentex, and amberjack, while the island's sheltered bays provide calm macro hunting year-round.

name: Lefkada
description: An Ionian island fringed by vivid blue caves, dramatic limestone cliffs, and sheltered bays teeming with Mediterranean marine life. Away from the busier Aegean crowds, Lefkada delivers intimate encounters with moray eels, grouper, octopus, nudibranchs, and seahorses across its diverse underwater terrain.

name: Romblon
description: Romblon sits in the Sibuyan Sea, one of the Philippines' best-kept macro diving secrets where thresher sharks, frogfish, and pygmy seahorses share the same pristine waters. Minimal tourist infrastructure means reefs remain in exceptional condition, drawing specialist underwater photographers from around the world.

name: Batangas
description: The Mabini Peninsula and surrounding Batangas waters extend well beyond the famous Anilao reefs, offering sites rich with seasnake feeding stations, resident turtles, Napoleon wrasse, and spectacular barracuda schools. Batangas is one of the most accessible year-round dive destinations from Manila, with a diversity of habitats ranging from sheltered bays to open-water pinnacles.

name: Trinidad
description: Trinidad's west and north coasts offer a different flavor of Caribbean diving — nutrient-rich upwelling from the Orinoco Delta draws tarpon, snook, and eagle rays into mangrove-fringed shores, while protected coral lagoons at Buccoo shelter hawksbill turtles year-round.

name: Los Testigos
description: Los Testigos is a remote Venezuelan archipelago rarely visited by divers, offering some of the most pristine and untouched reef ecosystems in the southeastern Caribbean, with whale sharks seasonally passing through and dense schools of barracuda circling deepwater seamounts.

name: Koh Phi Phi
description: Iconic limestone karst islands rising from turquoise water between Phuket and Krabi. Reef walls drop 30 m alongside granite boulders, leopard sharks rest on sandy plateaus year-round, and seasonal whale sharks pass through the channel during March and April. The shallows of Bida Nok and Bida Nai hold some of the healthiest hard coral cover in the Thai Andaman.

name: Koh Lipe
description: Thailand's southernmost inhabited island sits inside Tarutao National Marine Park, just 10 km from the Malaysian border. Undisturbed by liveaboard traffic, the reefs here have some of the healthiest coral gardens in the country. Staghorn and tabletop acropora dominate the shallows, while deeper slopes yield bumphead parrotfish, turtles, and occasional whale sharks between December and March.

name: Lombok
description: Sandwiched between Bali and Sumbawa, Lombok offers some of Indonesia's most dramatic underwater topography — sheer walls plunging beyond 40 metres, strong currents that funnel nutrients through the Lombok Strait, and a reliable cast of blacktip and whitetip reef sharks. The Gili Islands off the northwest coast are the main hub, with sandy-bottomed turtle cleaning stations and colourful reef crests sitting above the drops. Visibility regularly exceeds 25 metres and water temperatures stay warm year-round, making Lombok an accessible yet rewarding destination for intermediate and advanced divers alike.

name: Perhentian Islands
description: The Perhentian Islands sit in Malaysia's South China Sea roughly 20 kilometres off the Terengganu coast, ringed by crystal-clear water that turns an almost improbable shade of turquoise during the dry season. Visibility regularly reaches 20 metres or more over healthy hard-coral gardens patrolled by green and hawksbill turtles, blacktip reef sharks, and a dense cast of macro subjects including frogfish, nudibranchs, and ghost pipefish. The islands close entirely from November through February when northeast monsoon swells roll in, but from March through October conditions are ideal for snorkelling, reef dives, and night dives — making this one of the most approachable and affordable dive destinations in Southeast Asia.

name: Coral Bay
description: Coral Bay sits at the southern end of Ningaloo Reef, one of the longest fringing reefs on Earth. The reef here begins just metres from the beach, making it extraordinary for snorkellers and divers alike. Manta rays cruise the shallows year-round, and whale sharks arrive each autumn. The water is warm, clear, and alive with colour — parrotfish, turtles, and reef sharks share the same sandy-bottomed lagoon. This is a place where the ocean is genuinely wild and genuinely close.

name: Exmouth
description: Exmouth sits at the tip of the North West Cape, flanked by the open Indian Ocean to the west and the sheltered Exmouth Gulf to the east. The Navy Pier at Harold E. Holt Communication Station is considered one of the best shore dives in the world — a barnacle-encrusted structure alive with wobbegong sharks, giant groper, and nudibranchs at every depth. Ningaloo Reef wraps around the cape, delivering wall dives, drift dives, and the annual whale shark aggregation that draws divers from everywhere.

name: Bay of Islands
description: A subtropical archipelago of 144 islands off Northland's coast, the Bay of Islands blends temperate kelp forests with warm northern waters. Colorful nudibranchs coat every surface, common dolphins ride bow waves between dives, and the wreck of the HMNZS Canterbury sits upright on the seafloor — one of the Pacific's great wreck dives. Visibility is best in autumn when the summer plankton bloom settles.

name: Bora Bora
description: Bora Bora's turquoise lagoon is ringed by a coral barrier reef sheltering lemon sharks, blacktip reef sharks, and eagle rays that glide over coral bommies in shallow water. The passes cutting through the barrier — particularly Teavanui — funnel nutrient-rich currents that draw large pelagics. Visibility routinely exceeds 30 meters, and the calm lagoon makes it accessible to every level of diver.

name: Fakarava
description: Fakarava is a UNESCO biosphere reserve and one of the world's premier drift diving destinations. The South Pass (Tumakohua) is legendary for its wall of sharks — hundreds of grey reef sharks hunting in formation during the June grouper spawning aggregation. Two coral-fringed passes funnel nutrient-rich Pacific water through the atoll, creating powerful drift dives past healthy coral gardens teeming with reef fish, Napoleon wrasse, and eagle rays.

name: Moorea
description: Moorea is the heart-shaped island visible from Tahiti and a world-class shark and ray diving destination. Its lagoon shelters resident populations of blacktip and lemon sharks year-round, while spinner dolphins patrol the outer reef. Coral gardens in Cook's Bay and Opunohu Bay support dense populations of butterflyfish, surgeonfish, and moray eels, with healthy hard coral coverage making this one of French Polynesia's most photogenic dive locations.

name: Cartagena / Rosario Islands
description: Colombia's Caribbean gem where a 28-island coral archipelago meets colonial history, with vibrant reef fish, resident nurse sharks, and accessible wreck diving minutes from one of South America's most beautiful cities.

name: Manta / La Plata Island
description: Ecuador's Pacific gateway for giant oceanic manta rays, seasonal hammerhead aggregations, and whale watching, centred on La Plata Island — the 'poor man's Galapagos' — with year-round warm upwelling waters.

name: Cabo San Lucas
description: Where the Pacific meets the Sea of Cortez, Cabo San Lucas delivers world-class diving anchored by Land's End and the iconic El Arco arch. California sea lions haul out on the rocks year-round and pour into the water with divers at the colony. The famous underwater sand falls cascade off a submerged plateau at 30 m, and the open blue brings hammerheads, whale sharks, and manta rays. Jacques Cousteau called the Sea of Cortez 'the world's aquarium' — the southern tip is where you feel it first.

name: Puerto Vallarta
description: Puerto Vallarta anchors diving across Banderas Bay — one of the largest bays in the Americas — and the protected waters of Las Islas Marietas National Park just offshore. Giant manta rays cruise the seamounts, humpback whales fill the bay from November through March, and the Marietas hide a collapsed volcanic cave that shelters resting white sea birds and macro life. The bay's warm current pocket and diverse topography make it a year-round destination with genuinely different experiences by season.

name: Vancouver Island / Victoria
description: Cold, nutrient-rich Pacific waters off southern Vancouver Island host some of the world's largest invertebrates — giant Pacific octopus reaching 14 kg, wolf eels guarding rocky crevices, and massive sunflower sea stars. Kelp forests thick with lingcod and rockfish line the walls of Ogden Point and Race Rocks. Water temperatures hover between 7 and 12 C year-round, rewarding drysuit divers with extraordinary biomass and visibility that often reaches 20 m in winter.

name: San Juan Islands
description: The San Juan archipelago sits at the confluence of the Strait of Juan de Fuca and the Strait of Georgia, where tidal exchanges push nutrient-dense water through narrow passes at up to 8 knots. Resident and transient orca pods hunt these channels. Below the surface, giant Pacific octopus, wolf eels, and tube-dwelling anemones carpet rocky walls. Kelp canopies shelter copper rockfish and lingcod. This is cold-water Pacific Northwest diving at its most dramatic — drysuits mandatory, rewards extraordinary.

name: Nice / Cote d'Azur
description: The French Riviera hides a surprisingly rich underwater world beneath its glamorous surface. The Larvotto Marine Reserve just east of Monaco protects some of the densest grouper and sea fan populations on the northern Mediterranean coast, while canyon walls and rocky reefs around Cap Ferrat and the Lerin Islands shelter scorpionfish, moray eels, and clouds of chromis. Water clarity peaks in summer when thermoclines stack warm surface water above cold blue depths, extending visibility to 25 metres or more.

name: Puerto Princesa / Honda Bay
description: Honda Bay sits just north of Puerto Princesa city on Palawan island, sheltering a scatter of coral-ringed islands inside a sheltered lagoon. The reefs here are among the least disturbed in the Philippines, with nurse sharks resting under coral tables at Starfish Island, bumphead parrotfish grazing the outer slopes, and extensive seagrass meadows hosting dugongs and sea turtles. Visibility regularly exceeds 20 metres and currents are gentle, making this an ideal destination for all skill levels. The wider Palawan seascape sits within the Coral Triangle and holds some of the highest marine biodiversity on the planet.

name: Siargao
description: Siargao is best known as the Philippines' surf capital, but beneath the waves lies a world-class diving destination shaped by its position in the Coral Triangle. The Bucas Grande island group to the south contains Sohoton Cove, a network of tidal marine lakes and limestone caverns where endemic stingless jellyfish drift in brackish water. Outside the lake system, current-swept channels deliver schooling jacks, whale sharks in season, and pristine reef slopes draped with soft corals and sea fans. Macro photographers find outstanding subjects in the muck zones around Daco Island.

name: Ternate
description: A volcanic island in North Maluku where Gamalama's flanks plunge beneath the sea as sheer black-sand slopes and pristine reef walls. Ternate sits at the heart of the Coral Triangle with exceptional macro life on its muck sites and healthy walls draped in soft corals and sea fans. The island's remoteness keeps dive pressure low and biodiversity extraordinarily high.

name: Tufi
description: Tufi occupies a drowned river valley system in Oro Province where steep fjord walls drop into some of PNG's most pristine coral reef. WWII wrecks litter the seabed from the Cape Nelson campaign, and the sheltered fjords protect extraordinary biodiversity year-round. Tufi is regarded as one of the last genuinely untouched dive destinations in the Pacific.

name: Terceira
description: Rising from the mid-Atlantic Ridge, Terceira is one of the Azores archipelago's most volcanically active islands, and its underwater terrain reflects that restless geology — lava arches, hydrothermal vents, and seamounts thick with gorgonian sea fans. Blue sharks appear in numbers that surprise even seasoned pelagic divers, mobula rays sweep overhead in loose squadrons, and the cold clear Atlantic delivers visibility that can stretch beyond 30 metres. This is open-ocean diving at its most elemental.

name: Nusa Penida
description: Nusa Penida is Bali's offshore island and one of Indonesia's most thrilling dive destinations, famous worldwide as the best place on Earth to encounter the ocean sunfish (mola mola) from July through October. The island sits at the confluence of upwelling cold water from the deep Lombok Strait, fueling extraordinary marine productivity. Dramatic underwater topography — sheer walls dropping hundreds of metres, submerged pinnacles, and sweeping drift channels — combines with the nutrient-rich current to attract manta rays year-round, schooling fish in vast numbers, and the bizarre mola mola coming up from the deep to be cleaned. Currents here can be powerful and unpredictable, making many sites intermediate to advanced territory.

name: Verde Island Passage
description: The Verde Island Passage, a narrow strait between Luzon and Mindoro in Batangas province, holds the highest recorded concentration of marine shore fish species on Earth — a distinction scientists call the centre of the centre of marine biodiversity. Described by Dr. Kent Carpenter and colleagues in a landmark 2010 paper, the corridor hosts over 1,700 species of fish and extraordinary reef density compressed into accessible dive sites. Sheer walls plunge from the surface into blue water, while nutrient-rich currents sweep past schooling jacks, dogtooth tuna, and clouds of anthias. Macro life rivals anything in the Coral Triangle, and encounters with thresher sharks, hammerheads, and whale sharks are seasonal highlights.

name: Candidasa
description: Candidasa is a laid-back fishing village on Bali's east coast, sitting between the mola mola hotspot of Nusa Penida and the famous USAT Liberty wreck at Tulamben. The reefs here are less visited than Bali's tourist centers, rewarding divers with intact coral gardens, resident barracuda schools, and strong drift dives along offshore pinnacles. The nearby Gili Tepekong and Gili Mimpang islets concentrate big fish action — white-tip reef sharks, barracuda, and occasional mola mola pass through the channels. Water visibility is often excellent, and the cooler upwellings from the Lombok Strait keep the water rich in nutrients.

name: Nusa Lembongan
description: Nusa Lembongan and its sister islands Nusa Ceningan and Nusa Penida form a triangle of world-class diving just 30 minutes from Sanur. The area is famous globally for manta ray cleaning stations and seasonal oceanic mola mola (sunfish) sightings from July through October. Strong currents funneling through the channels create nutrient-rich upwellings that support extraordinary biodiversity — reef sharks, eagle rays, and dense schools of fish are routine. Drift dives here range from exhilarating to extreme, and the varied topography includes walls, pinnacles, and sloping reefs covered in hard coral.

name: Jervis Bay
description: Jervis Bay's gin-clear waters are among the cleanest in New South Wales, fed by a protected marine park that harbours one of Australia's highest densities of wobbegong sharks, resident grey nurse sharks in the sea caves, and seasonal manta ray aggregations. White sand seafloor bounces light back up through the water column, producing visibility that regularly tops 20 metres even in winter.

name: Byron Bay
description: Australia's most easterly point sits on the meeting line of warm tropical water pushing south down the East Australian Current and cooler temperate water coming north, making Byron Bay a year-round mixing zone for an extraordinary breadth of marine life. Julian Rocks marine reserve is the centrepiece, with grey nurse sharks aggregating in the gutters through winter and leopard sharks resting on the sand in summer. Common dolphins, loggerhead turtles and manta rays are regular visitors.

name: Shark Bay
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site protecting one of the world's largest seagrass meadows, Shark Bay harbours enormous dugong populations, loggerhead sea turtles, and the living stromatolites at Hamelin Pool — some of the oldest life forms on Earth. Tiger sharks patrol the shallows year-round, drawn by the abundant marine mammals. The bay's hypersaline conditions and extraordinary biodiversity make every dive a window into ancient and living ocean systems.

name: Cocos (Keeling) Islands
description: One of the most remote dive destinations in the world, the Cocos (Keeling) Islands sit 2750 km northwest of Perth in the eastern Indian Ocean. This pristine coral atoll delivers encounters with scalloped hammerheads, silvertip and grey reef sharks, manta rays, and schools of pelagic fish around near-untouched reef structures. With few visitors and no land-based dive industry, the underwater wilderness here is as raw and undisturbed as it gets in Australian waters.

name: Koh Samui & Ang Thong
description: Koh Samui sits in the Gulf of Thailand alongside the Ang Thong National Marine Park, an archipelago of 42 islands with emerald lagoons, limestone karsts, and fringing reefs. Diving here rewards with whale shark encounters from March through May, dense schools of snapper and fusiliers around pinnacles, and macro life tucked into rubble and soft coral gardens. The marine park's protected status keeps reef fish populations robust, and seasonal visibility peaks in the dry months of January through April.

name: Sail Rock & Southwest Pinnacle
description: Sail Rock, known locally as Hin Bai, rises from the Gulf of Thailand between Koh Tao and Koh Phangan and is widely regarded as the Gulf's premier dive site. A dramatic chimney runs through the rock allowing divers to ascend through a column of glassfish, while the surrounding walls are draped with seafans and patrolled by whale sharks from February through May. Southwest Pinnacle, a cluster of submerged peaks southwest of Koh Tao, draws schooling barracuda, trevally, and frequent whale shark sightings in the same season.

name: Osezaki
description: Osezaki sits at the western tip of Suruga Bay in Shizuoka Prefecture where the bay's deep cold-water upwelling collides with a rugged volcanic wall, producing one of Japan's most spectacular pelagic and reef experiences. Massive schools of fusiliers wheel in the blue water above the wall while whitetip reef sharks rest motionless on sand ledges below 20 metres. Soft corals drape the vertical rock face in orange and purple, and the nutrient-rich current draws regular visits from hammerhead sharks and open-water tuna. Shore entry and resident dive services make this one of Japan's most accessible big-animal sites.

name: Tateyama / Sagami Bay
description: Tateyama in southern Chiba Prefecture is the gateway to Sagami Bay, long regarded as Japan's nudibranch capital with over 150 species recorded from its rocky kelp reefs. Cold Oyashio-influenced water keeps the bay productive year-round, sustaining extraordinary densities of aeolid and dorid nudibranchs, ornate ghost pipefish, and the resident wobbegong sharks that drape themselves across rocky ledges. Winter water temperatures below 15 degrees Celsius reward cold-tolerant macro photographers with species rarely found anywhere else in the world, and spring brings migratory cuttlefish aggregations.

name: German Channel
description: German Channel is Palau's most celebrated manta ray destination, a dredged passage between the Rock Islands where oceanic manta rays gather at a permanent cleaning station year-round. Resident mantas return daily to be serviced by wrasse and butterflyfish, making predictable sightings almost guaranteed.
